planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February. Blue Chip requires approximately 200–300 chill hours — a very low requirement making it suitable for zone 9b and warmer parts of zone 9a. Cross-pollinate with another low-chill variety. The dwarf, compact growth habit makes Blue Chip excellent for container growing and small landscapes.

directions: Plant in full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), organically rich, well-drained, sandy soil; Blue Chip is a low-chill-hour dwarf southern highbush blueberry selected for compact growth habit — typically reaching only 3–4 feet tall and wide, making it the most container-appropriate blueberry variety and an excellent choice for small gardens, patios, and raised beds where standard blueberries are too large. The moderate-sized, sweet, firm berries ripen in May–June in zone 9 with good flavor and production relative to the small plant size. Blue Chip's dwarf habit is not a compromise in productivity — the plant concentrates its energy into fruit production rather than excessive vegetative growth, and a mature container-grown Blue Chip in a large pot can produce 2–4 pounds of fruit annually. The ornamental value is also high: the spring white flower clusters, glossy summer foliage, and brilliant orange-to-red fall color make Blue Chip a true four-season specimen for containers and small gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dwarf Blueberry Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Blue Chip Blueberry
University of Georgia Extension — Container-Grown Blueberries

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February. Rebel requires approximately 500–600 chill hours, suitable for zone 9a and the upper part of zone 9b. Cross-pollinate with another mid-chill variety for best production. In zone 9 Rebel ripens in May–June and produces large, high-quality fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), organically rich, well-drained, sandy soil; Rebel is a southern highbush blueberry developed by Fall Creek Farm & Nursery, released specifically for its combination of very large fruit size and excellent flavor — the berries are among the largest available in southern highbush blueberries, and their rich, sweet-tart, complex flavor rivals northern highbush varieties that are not adapted to zone 9 conditions. The vigorous, productive plant produces heavy crops of these exceptional large-caliber berries with a firm texture that holds up well in packing, shipping, and storage. Rebel's fruit quality has made it popular among commercial pick-your-own operations in the Southeast where premium large berries command top prices. In zone 9 home gardens it provides exceptional fresh eating quality — the large berries are satisfying to pick and eat fresh from the bush. Cross-pollinate with Flordablue, Biloxi, or Windsor for maximum crop set.
sources: Fall Creek Farm & Nursery — Rebel Blueberry Release Notes
North Carolina State University Extension — Rebel Southern Highbush Blueberry
University of Georgia Extension — Premium Southern Highbush Varieties

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Apache is a thornless, erect blackberry that requires no trellis support, though a simple post-and-wire system improves fruit quality and harvest ease. In zone 9 Apache ripens June–July. Prune all fruited canes to the ground after harvest; tie new primocanes loosely to trellis wires.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Apache is a thornless erect blackberry developed by the University of Arkansas as part of their world-leading blackberry breeding program. It produces very large, firm, sweet-tart, jet-black berries — among the largest of any thornless blackberry variety at 1–1.5 inch length and 8–12 grams per berry. The erect habit allows Apache to be grown without trellis support, though light support improves the crop. The exceptional fruit size and good flavor make Apache a top choice for fresh eating, farmers' markets, and home preserving. In zone 9 Apache performs well and is resistant to the orange rust fungus that can devastate susceptible blackberry varieties in the humid Gulf South. The thornless canes make harvest much more pleasant and efficient than thorny types, and the erect habit means berries are not dragged through the mud on trailing varieties.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Apache Blackberry Release
University of Georgia Extension — Apache Blackberry for Georgia
Mississippi State University Extension — Thornless Blackberry Varieties

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Osage is a thornless, semi-erect blackberry with a relatively compact habit compared to other erect varieties. In zone 9 it ripens in late May–June, one of the earlier erect thornless blackberries. Prune fruited canes to the ground after harvest and train new primocanes on a two-wire trellis system.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Osage is a University of Arkansas-released thornless semi-erect blackberry selected for early ripening (among the first thornless erect blackberries to ripen in June in zone 9) and exceptional flavor — rated by Arkansas evaluators as the best-flavored of their thornless erect releases, with a rich, sweet, complex blackberry flavor and balanced sugar-to-acid ratio that surpasses the milder flavor of some other University of Arkansas varieties. The large berries are firm, glossy, and jet-black with excellent shelf life after harvest. The early ripening of Osage is a meaningful advantage in zone 9 where the blackberry harvest window can be shortened by early summer heat — getting the bulk of the crop in late May to mid-June avoids the worst of the heat. In zone 9 Osage is planted alongside mid-season varieties like Apache to extend the harvest over a longer period.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Osage Blackberry Release
University of Georgia Extension — Early-Season Blackberry Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Thornless Erect Blackberries

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Von is a thorny, trailing blackberry that requires a sturdy trellis or fence for support. It ripens earlier than most erect varieties — May–June in zone 9. Prune fruited canes to the ground after harvest and train the long, flexible primocanes on horizontal wires.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Von is a trailing (dewberry-type) blackberry selected by the University of Georgia specifically for the Gulf Coast and Deep South, with exceptional heat tolerance and disease resistance suited to the humid conditions of the lower South. Unlike the erect University of Arkansas varieties that were developed for upland conditions, Von was selected specifically for performance in the hot, humid, rainy conditions of coastal Georgia, the Florida panhandle, and Gulf Coast zone 9. The large, sweet, flavorful berries ripen in May–June in zone 9, earlier than most erect varieties. Von requires a trellis or fence to support its trailing, vining habit, but the trailing type has the advantage of naturally positioning the fruiting lateral branches where they receive maximum sun exposure. Disease resistance to orange rust and rosette disease is an important selection criterion in zone 9 where these diseases can be serious problems for susceptible trailing blackberry varieties.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Von Blackberry Release Notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blackberry Varieties for Florida
Mississippi State University Extension — Gulf Coast Blackberry Production
